text_summary: The MAPK/ERK (RAS-RAF-MEK-ERK) pathway is one of the most frequently dysregulated signaling cascades in human cancers, with activating mutations found in approximately 40% of all solid tumors. Canonical signaling proceeds through receptor tyrosine kinase (RTK) activation of RAS GTPases (KRAS, NRAS, HRAS), which activate RAF kinases (BRAF, CRAF), leading to MEK1/2 phosphorylation and subsequent ERK1/2 activation. ERK1/2 phosphorylates transcription factors driving proliferation, survival, and differentiation. Key therapeutic nodes include BRAF (vemurafenib, dabrafenib, encorafenib), MEK (trametinib, cobimetinib, binimetinib, selumetinib), KRAS G12C (sotorasib, adagrasib), and upstream RTKs (EGFR, HER2). Feedback reactivation through RTK upregulation and RAF dimerization are major resistance mechanisms requiring combination vertical pathway blockade.
key_genes: KRAS, NRAS, HRAS, BRAF, CRAF, MAP2K1, MAP2K2, MAPK1, MAPK3, NF1
therapeutic_targets: BRAF (vemurafenib, dabrafenib), MEK (trametinib, binimetinib), KRAS G12C (sotorasib, adagrasib)
cross_talk: PI3K/AKT/mTOR, EGFR signaling, cell cycle (CDK4/6)
clinical_relevance: Mutated in ~40% of cancers. BRAF V600E in melanoma/NSCLC/CRC, KRAS in NSCLC/CRC/pancreatic, NF1 loss. Vertical BRAF+MEK combination is standard of care.

text_summary: The PI3K/AKT/mTOR pathway is a critical cell survival and metabolic signaling cascade frequently activated in cancer through PIK3CA activating mutations, PTEN loss, AKT amplification, or upstream RTK signaling. PI3K generates PIP3 from PIP2, recruiting AKT to the membrane for activation. Activated AKT promotes cell survival (inhibiting BAD, FOXO), growth (activating mTORC1), and metabolic reprogramming. PTEN is the primary negative regulator, dephosphorylating PIP3. mTOR exists in two complexes: mTORC1 (rapamycin-sensitive, regulates translation via S6K and 4EBP1) and mTORC2 (directly activates AKT). Therapeutic targets include PI3K-alpha (alpelisib), AKT (capivasertib), and mTOR (everolimus, temsirolimus). The pathway exhibits extensive cross-talk with MAPK through RAS and feedback loops, necessitating combination strategies in many contexts.
key_genes: PIK3CA, PIK3R1, PTEN, AKT1, AKT2, AKT3, MTOR, TSC1, TSC2, RICTOR, RPTOR, STK11
therapeutic_targets: PIK3CA (alpelisib), AKT (capivasertib), mTOR (everolimus, temsirolimus)
cross_talk: MAPK/ERK, EGFR signaling, cell cycle (CDK4/6), apoptosis (BCL-2)
clinical_relevance: PIK3CA mutations in ~40% HR+ breast cancer (alpelisib approved). PTEN loss pan-cancer. mTOR inhibitors in renal cell and neuroendocrine tumors. Capivasertib + fulvestrant in AKT-pathway-altered breast cancer.

text_summary: EGFR (ErbB1/HER1) signaling is a receptor tyrosine kinase pathway that activates MAPK, PI3K/AKT, JAK/STAT, and PLCgamma cascades upon ligand binding (EGF, TGF-alpha) and receptor dimerization. EGFR is a key oncogenic driver in NSCLC (activating mutations in 15-20%), head and neck cancer (overexpression), colorectal cancer (wild-type is required for anti-EGFR antibody efficacy), and glioblastoma (amplification and EGFRvIII). The ErbB family includes four members: EGFR, HER2, HER3, and HER4, which form homo- and heterodimers with distinct signaling outputs. Therapeutic strategies span small-molecule TKIs (osimertinib, erlotinib, gefitinib for mutant EGFR), monoclonal antibodies (cetuximab, panitumumab for wild-type EGFR/RAS in CRC), bispecific antibodies (amivantamab targeting EGFR+MET), and third-generation agents for resistance mutations.
key_genes: EGFR, ERBB2, ERBB3, ERBB4, GRB2, SOS1, SHC1, GAB1, PTPN11
therapeutic_targets: EGFR (osimertinib, cetuximab, amivantamab), HER2 (trastuzumab), pan-ErbB (afatinib)
cross_talk: MAPK/ERK, PI3K/AKT/mTOR, JAK/STAT, MET signaling
clinical_relevance: EGFR mutations in 15-20% NSCLC (osimertinib standard 1L). Wild-type EGFR/RAS required for cetuximab in CRC. HER2 amplification in breast/gastric. EGFRvIII in glioblastoma.

text_summary: The WNT/beta-catenin pathway is a fundamental developmental signaling cascade that is constitutively activated in the majority of colorectal cancers through APC truncating mutations (~80%) or CTNNB1 (beta-catenin) activating mutations. In the absence of WNT ligand, beta-catenin is phosphorylated by the APC/Axin/GSK3-beta/CK1 destruction complex and targeted for proteasomal degradation. APC loss prevents beta-catenin degradation, leading to its nuclear accumulation and transcriptional activation of TCF/LEF target genes (MYC, CCND1, AXIN2) driving proliferation and stem cell self-renewal. Therapeutic targeting of WNT signaling has been challenging due to the pathway's essential role in normal tissue homeostasis. Emerging approaches include tankyrase inhibitors (stabilizing the destruction complex), porcupine inhibitors (blocking WNT ligand secretion), and beta-catenin/TCF interaction disruptors.
key_genes: APC, CTNNB1, AXIN1, AXIN2, GSK3B, TCF7L2, RNF43, ZNRF3, RSPO2, RSPO3
therapeutic_targets: Tankyrase (tankyrase inhibitors), porcupine (WNT974), beta-catenin/TCF (investigational)
cross_talk: Hippo/YAP, Notch, Hedgehog, PI3K/AKT/mTOR
clinical_relevance: APC mutations in ~80% CRC, driving the adenoma-carcinoma sequence. CTNNB1 mutations in hepatocellular carcinoma, desmoid tumors. RNF43/RSPO fusions in CRC subset. No approved WNT-targeting drugs yet.

text_summary: The DNA Damage Response (DDR) and BRCA-mediated homologous recombination (HR) pathway is a critical genome maintenance system that repairs DNA double-strand breaks with high fidelity. Key components include damage sensors (ATM, ATR), signal transducers (CHK1, CHK2), and effectors (BRCA1, BRCA2, PALB2, RAD51). BRCA1 and BRCA2 are essential for RAD51-mediated strand invasion during homologous recombination. Loss of HR function (through BRCA1/2 mutations, PALB2 mutations, or other HRD mechanisms) forces reliance on error-prone repair pathways (NHEJ, MMEJ), creating synthetic lethality with PARP inhibition. PARP inhibitors (olaparib, rucaparib, niraparib, talazoparib) trap PARP1 on DNA, generating replication-associated double-strand breaks that are lethal in HRD-deficient cells. DDR pathway alterations are found across multiple cancer types and define a pan-cancer population eligible for PARP-based therapies.
key_genes: BRCA1, BRCA2, PALB2, ATM, ATR, CHEK1, CHEK2, RAD51, RAD51C, RAD51D, BARD1, BRIP1, NBN, MRE11
therapeutic_targets: PARP (olaparib, rucaparib, niraparib, talazoparib), ATR (ceralasertib), WEE1 (adavosertib), CHK1
cross_talk: Cell cycle (CDK4/6), apoptosis (BCL-2, p53), PI3K/AKT
clinical_relevance: BRCA1/2 mutations in ovarian, breast, prostate, pancreatic cancers. HRD score (myChoice CDx) identifies non-BRCA HRD tumors. PARP inhibitors are standard of care in BRCA/HRD-positive cancers.

text_summary: The cell cycle pathway regulated by cyclin-dependent kinases (CDK4/6) controls the G1-to-S phase transition through phosphorylation of the retinoblastoma protein (RB1). Cyclin D1 (CCND1) complexes with CDK4/6 to initiate RB1 hyperphosphorylation, releasing E2F transcription factors that drive S-phase entry. CDK4/6 inhibitors (palbociclib, ribociclib, abemaciclib) have transformed HR-positive/HER2-negative breast cancer treatment, with PFS improvements in combination with endocrine therapy. The PALOMA-2 trial (palbociclib + letrozole: PFS 24.8 months), MONALEESA-2/3/7 (ribociclib: OS benefit in multiple settings), and monarchE (abemaciclib: adjuvant DFS benefit) established CDK4/6 inhibitors as standard of care. Loss of RB1, CDKN2A deletion, and cyclin E1 amplification are resistance mechanisms. CDK4/6 inhibition also promotes anti-tumor immunity through type III interferon signaling.
key_genes: CDK4, CDK6, CCND1, CCND2, CCND3, CDKN2A, CDKN2B, RB1, CCNE1, CDK2, E2F1
therapeutic_targets: CDK4/6 (palbociclib, ribociclib, abemaciclib), CDK2 (investigational), CDK7 (investigational)
cross_talk: MAPK/ERK (CCND1 induction), PI3K/AKT/mTOR, DDR/BRCA, estrogen receptor signaling
clinical_relevance: CDK4/6 inhibitors are standard 1L for HR+/HER2- metastatic breast cancer. CDKN2A deletion in mesothelioma, glioblastoma. RB1 loss predicts resistance. Cyclin E amplification defines CDK4/6i-resistant population.

text_summary: The JAK/STAT (Janus Kinase/Signal Transducer and Activator of Transcription) pathway mediates cytokine receptor signaling and is central to hematopoiesis, immune regulation, and inflammation. JAK family kinases (JAK1, JAK2, JAK3, TYK2) are activated by cytokine receptor engagement and phosphorylate STAT proteins (STAT1-6), which dimerize, translocate to the nucleus, and activate transcription. Constitutive JAK/STAT activation is a hallmark of myeloproliferative neoplasms (JAK2 V617F mutation in ~95% polycythemia vera, ~55% essential thrombocythemia and myelofibrosis). JAK inhibitors (ruxolitinib, fedratinib, pacritinib) are approved for myelofibrosis. JAK/STAT signaling also plays roles in lymphoma, leukemia, and solid tumors. The pathway intersects with interferon signaling and can modulate tumor immune evasion. STAT3 is being investigated as a direct therapeutic target in solid tumors.
key_genes: JAK1, JAK2, JAK3, TYK2, STAT1, STAT3, STAT5A, STAT5B, SOCS1, SOCS3
therapeutic_targets: JAK1/2 (ruxolitinib), JAK2 (fedratinib, pacritinib), STAT3 (investigational)
cross_talk: MAPK/ERK, PI3K/AKT/mTOR, interferon signaling, NF-kB
clinical_relevance: JAK2 V617F in myeloproliferative neoplasms. JAK inhibitors approved for myelofibrosis and polycythemia vera. JAK/STAT dysregulation in lymphoma, T-ALL, and solid tumor immune evasion.

text_summary: The Hedgehog (Hh) signaling pathway is a developmental morphogen pathway that, when aberrantly activated, drives tumorigenesis in basal cell carcinoma (BCC), medulloblastoma, and rhabdomyosarcoma. Canonical Hh signaling involves the ligands Sonic Hedgehog (SHH), Indian Hedgehog, and Desert Hedgehog binding to the receptor Patched (PTCH1), which relieves PTCH1-mediated inhibition of Smoothened (SMO). Activated SMO promotes GLI transcription factor (GLI1, GLI2, GLI3) nuclear translocation and target gene expression. In BCC, PTCH1 loss-of-function mutations (~90%) or SMO gain-of-function mutations (~10%) constitutively activate the pathway. Vismodegib and sonidegib are FDA-approved SMO inhibitors for advanced BCC. Gorlin syndrome (nevoid BCC syndrome) results from germline PTCH1 mutations. Resistance to SMO inhibitors occurs through SMO D473H/Y mutations and GLI amplification.
key_genes: SHH, PTCH1, PTCH2, SMO, GLI1, GLI2, GLI3, SUFU
therapeutic_targets: SMO (vismodegib, sonidegib), GLI (investigational)
cross_talk: WNT/beta-catenin, Notch, PI3K/AKT/mTOR
clinical_relevance: PTCH1 mutations in ~90% BCC, SMO mutations in ~10%. Vismodegib/sonidegib approved for advanced BCC. Gorlin syndrome (germline PTCH1). Hh signaling in medulloblastoma SHH subtype.

text_summary: The Notch signaling pathway is a juxtacrine signaling system where Notch receptors (NOTCH1-4) on one cell interact with ligands (DLL1, DLL3, DLL4, JAG1, JAG2) on adjacent cells, triggering proteolytic cleavage by gamma-secretase and release of the Notch intracellular domain (NICD), which translocates to the nucleus and activates transcription of HES and HEY family genes. NOTCH1 activating mutations are the hallmark of T-cell acute lymphoblastic leukemia (T-ALL, >50%). In solid tumors, Notch can function as either an oncogene or tumor suppressor depending on context. NOTCH1/2 loss-of-function mutations occur in ~15% of squamous cell cancers. DLL3 is a tumor-associated antigen in small cell lung cancer (SCLC) and neuroendocrine tumors, targeted by the bispecific T-cell engager tarlatamab. Gamma-secretase inhibitors (nirogacestat for desmoid tumors) represent a therapeutic approach for Notch-dependent tumors.
key_genes: NOTCH1, NOTCH2, NOTCH3, NOTCH4, DLL1, DLL3, DLL4, JAG1, JAG2, FBXW7, HES1
therapeutic_targets: Gamma-secretase (nirogacestat), DLL3 (tarlatamab, rovalpituzumab), NOTCH (brontictuzumab)
cross_talk: WNT/beta-catenin, Hedgehog, Hippo/YAP, NF-kB
clinical_relevance: NOTCH1 activating mutations in T-ALL. DLL3-targeting in SCLC. Gamma-secretase inhibitor approved for desmoid tumors. Context-dependent oncogene vs tumor suppressor in solid tumors.

text_summary: The Hippo/YAP pathway is a growth-suppressive signaling cascade that controls organ size, tissue homeostasis, and stem cell biology. The core kinase cascade involves MST1/2 (STK3/4) phosphorylating LATS1/2, which phosphorylates the transcriptional co-activators YAP (YAP1) and TAZ (WWTR1), leading to their cytoplasmic sequestration and degradation. When Hippo signaling is inactivated, unphosphorylated YAP/TAZ enter the nucleus and interact with TEAD transcription factors to drive proliferation, survival, and epithelial-mesenchymal transition. NF2 (Merlin) loss is a key activator of YAP/TAZ in mesothelioma and schwannoma. Hippo pathway dysregulation is implicated in liver, stomach, colorectal, and lung cancers. TEAD inhibitors and YAP-TEAD interaction disruptors are in early clinical development. Cross-talk with WNT and Notch pathways amplifies oncogenic signaling in multiple contexts.
key_genes: NF2, LATS1, LATS2, STK3, STK4, YAP1, WWTR1, TEAD1, TEAD2, TEAD3, TEAD4, MOB1A, SAV1
therapeutic_targets: TEAD (TEAD inhibitors, investigational), YAP/TAZ (investigational)
cross_talk: WNT/beta-catenin, Notch, EGFR signaling, cell cycle
clinical_relevance: NF2 loss in mesothelioma, schwannoma, meningioma. YAP/TAZ activation pan-cancer. TEAD inhibitors in early clinical trials. YAP amplification in NSCLC and liver cancer.

text_summary: The apoptosis pathway regulated by the BCL-2 protein family controls the intrinsic (mitochondrial) cell death program. Pro-survival BCL-2 family members (BCL-2, BCL-XL, MCL-1) sequester pro-apoptotic BH3-only proteins (BIM, BAD, BID, NOXA, PUMA) and effectors (BAX, BAK), preventing mitochondrial outer membrane permeabilization (MOMP) and cytochrome c release. Venetoclax is a selective BCL-2 inhibitor approved for CLL and AML that mimics BH3-only proteins to displace BAX/BAK. In CLL, venetoclax plus obinutuzumab achieved deep remissions with undetectable minimal residual disease. In AML, venetoclax plus azacitidine improved survival in elderly patients. MCL-1 and BCL-XL inhibitors are in development but face toxicity challenges. BCL-2 overexpression in follicular lymphoma (t(14;18)) and CLL drives resistance to apoptosis. p53 pathway defects and anti-apoptotic protein upregulation are major resistance mechanisms.
key_genes: BCL2, BCL2L1, MCL1, BAX, BAK1, BIM, BAD, BID, NOXA, PUMA, APAF1, CASP9, CASP3
therapeutic_targets: BCL-2 (venetoclax), MCL-1 (investigational), BCL-XL (investigational), IAP (investigational)
cross_talk: p53/MDM2, PI3K/AKT, DDR/BRCA, NF-kB
clinical_relevance: Venetoclax approved for CLL (frontline and R/R) and AML (with azacitidine). BCL-2 overexpression from t(14;18) in follicular lymphoma. MCL-1 dependence in multiple myeloma.

text_summary: The angiogenesis pathway driven by VEGF (Vascular Endothelial Growth Factor) signaling is essential for tumor neovascularization, providing oxygen and nutrients for tumor growth beyond 1-2mm. VEGF-A binds VEGFR2 (KDR) on endothelial cells, activating PI3K, MAPK, and PLCgamma cascades that promote endothelial proliferation, migration, and survival. Hypoxia-inducible factor (HIF) transcriptionally upregulates VEGF under low-oxygen conditions, creating a positive feedback loop in tumors. Anti-angiogenic therapies include the anti-VEGF antibody bevacizumab (approved in CRC, NSCLC, ovarian, cervical, HCC, renal), multi-kinase VEGFR inhibitors (sunitinib, sorafenib, pazopanib, cabozantinib, lenvatinib), and the VEGF trap aflibercept. In renal cell carcinoma, VHL loss leads to constitutive HIF activation and VEGF overexpression, making anti-VEGF therapy particularly effective. Combination of anti-VEGF with immunotherapy (pembrolizumab+lenvatinib, atezolizumab+bevacizumab) is a standard approach.
key_genes: VEGFA, VEGFB, VEGFC, KDR, FLT1, FLT4, HIF1A, EPAS1, VHL, ANGPT1, ANGPT2, TIE2
therapeutic_targets: VEGF (bevacizumab), VEGFR (sunitinib, pazopanib, cabozantinib, lenvatinib), HIF-2alpha (belzutifan)
cross_talk: MAPK/ERK, PI3K/AKT/mTOR, Hippo/YAP, Notch (tip/stalk cell specification)
clinical_relevance: Bevacizumab approved in CRC, NSCLC, ovarian, renal, HCC. VHL loss in clear cell RCC drives VEGF. HIF-2alpha inhibitor belzutifan approved for VHL disease. Anti-VEGF + immunotherapy combinations are standard in renal and HCC.

text_summary: The Hippo/YAP-TAZ pathway is a growth-suppressive signaling cascade that controls organ size, tissue homeostasis, and stem cell biology through regulation of the transcriptional co-activators YAP1 and TAZ (WWTR1). The core kinase cascade involves MST1/2 (STK3/4) phosphorylating LATS1/2 kinases, which phosphorylate YAP/TAZ to promote their cytoplasmic sequestration by 14-3-3 proteins and proteasomal degradation. When Hippo signaling is inactivated, unphosphorylated YAP/TAZ translocate to the nucleus and interact with TEAD1-4 transcription factors to drive expression of proliferative and anti-apoptotic genes including CTGF, CYR61, and BIRC5. NF2 (Merlin) loss is a key mechanism of YAP/TAZ activation in mesothelioma, schwannoma, and meningioma. YAP/TAZ activation through non-canonical mechanisms (mechanical signaling, GPCR signaling, metabolic cues) is implicated in liver, gastric, colorectal, and lung cancers. TEAD inhibitors and YAP-TEAD interaction disruptors are in early clinical development. Cross-talk with WNT, Notch, and EGFR pathways amplifies oncogenic YAP/TAZ signaling.
key_genes: NF2, LATS1, LATS2, STK3, STK4, YAP1, WWTR1, TEAD1, TEAD2, TEAD3, TEAD4, MOB1A, SAV1, VGLL4
therapeutic_targets: TEAD (TEAD inhibitors, investigational), YAP-TEAD PPI disruptors (investigational), SHP2 (upstream)
cross_talk: WNT/beta-catenin, Notch, EGFR signaling, mechanical signaling (integrin/FAK), GPCR signaling
clinical_relevance: NF2 loss in mesothelioma, schwannoma, meningioma. YAP amplification in NSCLC and liver cancer. TEAD inhibitors in early clinical trials. Extensive cross-talk makes Hippo a hub for multiple oncogenic pathways.

text_summary: The NF-kB (Nuclear Factor kappa-light-chain-enhancer of activated B cells) signaling pathway is a master regulator of inflammation, innate immunity, and cell survival that is constitutively activated in many cancers. The canonical pathway involves IKK complex (IKK-alpha, IKK-beta, NEMO/IKK-gamma) phosphorylation of IkB-alpha, leading to its proteasomal degradation and nuclear translocation of p65/p50 NF-kB dimers. The non-canonical pathway involves NIK (MAP3K14) and IKK-alpha-mediated processing of p100 to p52, activating p52/RelB dimers. NF-kB drives expression of anti-apoptotic genes (BCL-2, BCL-XL, XIAP), pro-inflammatory cytokines (IL-6, TNF-alpha), and immune-modulatory factors. Constitutive NF-kB activation occurs in DLBCL (ABC subtype via CARD11/BCL10/MALT1 signaling), multiple myeloma, Hodgkin lymphoma, and many solid tumors. Therapeutic targets include BTK inhibitors (ibrutinib in B-cell malignancies, which reduces NF-kB signaling), proteasome inhibitors (bortezomib, which prevents IkB degradation), and direct IKK-beta inhibitors under development.
key_genes: NFKB1, RELA, NFKB2, RELB, REL, NFKBIA, IKBKB, IKBKG, CHUK, MAP3K14, CARD11, BCL10, MALT1, MYD88, TNFAIP3
therapeutic_targets: Proteasome (bortezomib), BTK (ibrutinib), IKK-beta (investigational), MALT1 (investigational)
cross_talk: JAK/STAT, PI3K/AKT/mTOR, MAPK/ERK, Toll-like receptor signaling, TNF receptor signaling
clinical_relevance: Constitutive NF-kB in ABC-DLBCL, multiple myeloma, Hodgkin lymphoma. MYD88 L265P in Waldenstrom macroglobulinemia. Bortezomib mechanism partly through NF-kB inhibition. Ibrutinib reduces NF-kB in CLL/MCL.

text_summary: The TGF-beta (Transforming Growth Factor-beta) signaling pathway plays a dual role in cancer, acting as a tumor suppressor in early-stage disease and as a pro-metastatic factor in advanced cancers. Canonical signaling involves TGF-beta ligands (TGFB1, TGFB2, TGFB3) binding type II receptors (TGFBR2), which recruit and phosphorylate type I receptors (TGFBR1/ALK5). Activated TGFBR1 phosphorylates SMAD2/3, which complex with SMAD4 and translocate to the nucleus to regulate gene expression. In early cancers, TGF-beta induces growth arrest and apoptosis (tumor-suppressive). SMAD4 loss (present in 55% of pancreatic cancers) disables the tumor-suppressive arm. In advanced cancers, TGF-beta promotes epithelial-mesenchymal transition (EMT), immune evasion (suppressing CD8+ T cells, promoting Tregs), angiogenesis, and metastasis. Therapeutic approaches include TGF-beta ligand traps, receptor kinase inhibitors, and anti-TGF-beta antibodies. The immunosuppressive role of TGF-beta makes it an attractive combination target with checkpoint inhibitors.
key_genes: TGFB1, TGFB2, TGFB3, TGFBR1, TGFBR2, SMAD2, SMAD3, SMAD4, SMAD7, ACVR1, BMP2, BMP4
therapeutic_targets: TGFBR1 (galunisertib), TGF-beta ligand (fresolimumab), bifunctional anti-PD-L1/TGF-beta trap (bintrafusp alfa)
cross_talk: MAPK/ERK, PI3K/AKT, WNT/beta-catenin, Hippo/YAP, EMT regulation
clinical_relevance: SMAD4 loss in 55% of pancreatic cancers. TGF-beta drives EMT and immune evasion. Anti-TGF-beta + checkpoint inhibitor combinations in clinical trials. TGF-beta signature predicts immunotherapy resistance in some contexts.

text_summary: Epigenetic regulation through the EZH2/SWI-SNF axis represents a critical balance between gene silencing and gene activation in cancer. EZH2, the catalytic subunit of Polycomb Repressive Complex 2 (PRC2), trimethylates histone H3 at lysine 27 (H3K27me3), silencing tumor suppressor genes. The SWI/SNF chromatin remodeling complex (including SMARCB1/INI1, SMARCA4/BRG1, ARID1A, PBRM1) opposes PRC2 by promoting open chromatin and gene activation. Mutations in SWI/SNF subunits occur in approximately 20% of all cancers, creating a dependency on EZH2 activity (synthetic lethality). SMARCB1 loss (epithelioid sarcoma, rhabdoid tumors) and ARID1A loss (ovarian clear cell, endometrial, gastric cancers) result in unopposed EZH2-mediated repression. Gain-of-function EZH2 mutations (Y646N/F/H in follicular lymphoma) increase H3K27me3. Tazemetostat (EZH2 inhibitor) is FDA-approved for EZH2-mutant follicular lymphoma and SMARCB1-negative epithelioid sarcoma. This axis exemplifies therapeutic exploitation of epigenetic vulnerabilities.
key_genes: EZH2, EED, SUZ12, SMARCB1, SMARCA4, ARID1A, ARID1B, PBRM1, ARID2, BAP1, DNMT3A, TET2, IDH1, IDH2
therapeutic_targets: EZH2 (tazemetostat), IDH1 (ivosidenib), IDH2 (enasidenib), DNMT (azacitidine, decitabine), HDAC (vorinostat, romidepsin)
cross_talk: PI3K/AKT (through PTEN silencing), WNT/beta-catenin, Hippo/YAP, DNA damage response
clinical_relevance: SWI/SNF mutations in ~20% of all cancers. EZH2 mutations in FL, DLBCL. SMARCB1 loss in epithelioid sarcoma, rhabdoid tumors. ARID1A loss in ovarian clear cell, endometrial. Tazemetostat approved for EZH2-mutant FL and SMARCB1-loss sarcoma.

text_summary: The tumor immune microenvironment (TIME) encompasses the complex ecosystem of immune cells, stromal cells, vasculature, and extracellular matrix that interact with tumor cells to either promote or suppress anti-tumor immunity. Key immune populations include cytotoxic CD8+ T cells, helper CD4+ T cells, regulatory T cells (Tregs), natural killer (NK) cells, dendritic cells (DCs), tumor-associated macrophages (TAMs), and myeloid-derived suppressor cells (MDSCs). Immune checkpoint molecules (PD-1, PD-L1, CTLA-4, LAG-3, TIM-3, TIGIT) regulate the balance between immune activation and suppression. Hot tumors (high T-cell infiltration, PD-L1-positive) tend to respond to checkpoint inhibitors, while cold tumors (low infiltration, immune-excluded) are resistant. TIME classification informs immunotherapy strategy: inflamed tumors benefit from anti-PD-1/PD-L1, immune-excluded tumors may benefit from anti-angiogenics plus immunotherapy, and immune-desert tumors require priming strategies (oncolytic viruses, STING agonists, vaccines). The TIME is shaped by tumor genetics, with MAPK activation, WNT/beta-catenin, and STK11 loss driving immune exclusion.
key_genes: CD274, PDCD1, CTLA4, LAG3, HAVCR2, TIGIT, CD8A, CD4, FOXP3, IFNG, CXCL9, CXCL10, CD68, ARG1, IDO1
therapeutic_targets: PD-1 (pembrolizumab, nivolumab), PD-L1 (atezolizumab), CTLA-4 (ipilimumab), LAG-3 (relatlimab), DLL3 (tarlatamab)
cross_talk: MAPK/ERK (immune exclusion), WNT/beta-catenin (cold TME), TGF-beta (immune suppression), VEGF (immune modulation), cGAS-STING (innate immunity)
clinical_relevance: TIME classification guides immunotherapy selection. PD-L1, TMB, MSI as immune biomarkers. STK11/KEAP1 loss drives cold TME in NSCLC. Combination IO strategies for cold tumors. Bispecific T-cell engagers bypass TME barriers.

text_summary: Metabolic reprogramming, exemplified by the Warburg effect, is a hallmark of cancer wherein tumor cells preferentially utilize aerobic glycolysis (glycolysis followed by lactate production even in the presence of oxygen) over oxidative phosphorylation. This metabolic shift is driven by oncogenic signaling (MYC, HIF-1alpha, PI3K/AKT/mTOR, KRAS) and provides biosynthetic precursors for rapid proliferation. Key metabolic alterations include upregulated glucose transporters (GLUT1/SLC2A1), hexokinase 2 (HK2), lactate dehydrogenase A (LDHA), and glutaminase (GLS1). IDH1/2 mutations produce the oncometabolite D-2-hydroxyglutarate, blocking differentiation. The tumor metabolic microenvironment (acidosis, hypoxia, nutrient competition) suppresses anti-tumor immunity by impairing T-cell function. Therapeutic targets include IDH1/2 (ivosidenib, enasidenib), glutaminase (telaglenastat), and metabolic dependencies in specific contexts. PET imaging (FDG uptake) exploits the Warburg effect for cancer diagnosis and staging. Metformin and dietary interventions are under investigation as metabolic modulators in cancer.
key_genes: SLC2A1, HK2, PKM, LDHA, MYC, HIF1A, IDH1, IDH2, GLS, PHGDH, SLC1A5, ACLY, FASN
therapeutic_targets: IDH1 (ivosidenib), IDH2 (enasidenib), glutaminase (telaglenastat, investigational), FASN (TVB-2640, investigational)
cross_talk: PI3K/AKT/mTOR (metabolic regulation), AMPK/STK11, HIF (hypoxia response), MYC, MAPK/ERK
clinical_relevance: Warburg effect exploited by FDG-PET imaging. IDH inhibitors in AML and cholangiocarcinoma. Metabolic immunosuppression limits immunotherapy efficacy. Glutaminase inhibitors in clinical trials. Dietary interventions (ketogenic diet) under investigation.

text_summary: The ferroptosis pathway is a recently characterized form of regulated cell death driven by iron-dependent lipid peroxidation, distinct from apoptosis, necroptosis, and autophagy. Ferroptosis occurs when the balance between lipid peroxidation (driven by iron, polyunsaturated fatty acids, and lipoxygenases) overwhelms the cellular antioxidant defense (GPX4, the cystine/glutamate antiporter xCT/SLC7A11, and the CoQ10-FSP1 system). GPX4 (glutathione peroxidase 4) is the central ferroptosis suppressor, converting lipid hydroperoxides to non-toxic lipid alcohols. Ferroptosis sensitivity is influenced by tumor genotype: therapy-resistant mesenchymal cancer cells, drug-tolerant persister cells, and cells undergoing EMT show increased ferroptosis vulnerability. Cancer cells with high levels of polyunsaturated fatty acid incorporation in membrane phospholipids are particularly susceptible. Ferroptosis inducers (RSL3, erastin, FINs) are under preclinical development. Immunogenic cell death through ferroptosis may enhance anti-tumor immunity. The pathway intersects with iron metabolism, cysteine metabolism, and lipid metabolism.
key_genes: GPX4, SLC7A11, ACSL4, LPCAT3, ALOX15, TFRC, FTH1, FTL, NFE2L2, HMOX1, FSP1, DHODH
therapeutic_targets: GPX4 (RSL3, investigational), xCT/SLC7A11 (erastin, sulfasalazine), NRF2 (brusatol, investigational), iron metabolism
cross_talk: NRF2/KEAP1 (antioxidant defense), p53 (SLC7A11 repression), AMPK, lipid metabolism, iron metabolism
clinical_relevance: Drug-tolerant persister cells and therapy-resistant mesenchymal cancers are ferroptosis-vulnerable. GPX4 inhibitors in preclinical development. Ferroptosis may enhance immunotherapy through immunogenic cell death. NRF2/KEAP1 mutations in NSCLC modulate ferroptosis sensitivity.

text_summary: The cGAS-STING (Cyclic GMP-AMP Synthase - Stimulator of Interferon Genes) pathway is a critical innate immune sensing mechanism that detects cytosolic double-stranded DNA and activates type I interferon responses. cGAS binds cytosolic dsDNA (from tumor cells, damaged DNA, micronuclei, or pathogens) and catalyzes synthesis of the second messenger 2'3'-cGAMP, which binds and activates STING (TMEM173) on the endoplasmic reticulum. Activated STING recruits TBK1, which phosphorylates IRF3, driving transcription of type I interferons (IFN-alpha, IFN-beta) and pro-inflammatory cytokines. This pathway bridges innate and adaptive immunity by promoting dendritic cell maturation and cross-priming of CD8+ T cells against tumor antigens. Tumors often suppress cGAS-STING through STING silencing, cGAS degradation, or expression of the 2'3'-cGAMP hydrolase ENPP1. STING agonists (diABZI, MIW815/ADU-S100, E7766) and ENPP1 inhibitors are in clinical development as intratumoral and systemic immunotherapy agents, particularly for immunologically cold tumors resistant to checkpoint inhibitors.
key_genes: CGAS, TMEM173, TBK1, IRF3, IFNB1, ENPP1, TREX1, PRKDC, ATM
therapeutic_targets: STING agonists (diABZI, MIW815, E7766, investigational), ENPP1 inhibitors (investigational)
cross_talk: Type I interferon signaling, NF-kB, DNA damage response, autophagy, tumor immune microenvironment
clinical_relevance: cGAS-STING activation bridges innate and adaptive immunity. STING agonists in clinical trials for cold tumors. Radiation therapy activates cGAS-STING through micronuclei formation. Chromosomal instability activates cGAS-STING. STING silencing is a tumor immune evasion mechanism.

text_summary: The androgen receptor (AR) signaling pathway is the central oncogenic driver in prostate cancer, mediating testosterone and dihydrotestosterone (DHT) signaling through nuclear receptor transcription factor activity. Upon ligand binding, AR dissociates from heat shock proteins (HSP90), dimerizes, translocates to the nucleus, and binds androgen response elements (AREs) to activate transcription of target genes promoting proliferation (KLK3/PSA, TMPRSS2, NKX3-1, FKBP5) and survival. Androgen deprivation therapy (ADT) through surgical or chemical castration (GnRH agonists/antagonists) is the foundation of prostate cancer treatment. Castration-resistant prostate cancer (CRPC) develops through multiple AR-dependent mechanisms: AR gene amplification (30-40% of CRPC), AR point mutations broadening ligand specificity (T878A, L702H), AR splice variants lacking the ligand-binding domain (AR-V7), intratumoral androgen synthesis (CYP17A1), and AR-independent lineage plasticity (neuroendocrine differentiation). Second-generation AR pathway inhibitors target distinct nodes: enzalutamide and apalutamide (AR antagonists), abiraterone (CYP17A1 inhibitor blocking androgen synthesis), and darolutamide (AR antagonist with reduced CNS penetration). AR pathway cross-talk with PI3K/AKT (reciprocal feedback inhibition) provides rationale for combination strategies.
key_genes: AR, KLK3, TMPRSS2, NKX3-1, FKBP5, CYP17A1, HSD3B1, SRD5A1, SRD5A2, FOXA1, HOXB13, RB1, TP53
therapeutic_targets: AR (enzalutamide, apalutamide, darolutamide), CYP17A1 (abiraterone), GnRH (leuprolide, degarelix), AR degraders (investigational)
cross_talk: PI3K/AKT/mTOR (reciprocal feedback), MAPK/ERK, WNT/beta-catenin (lineage plasticity), cell cycle (RB1/TP53 loss drives NE differentiation)
clinical_relevance: AR pathway is the central target in prostate cancer. Enzalutamide, abiraterone, apalutamide, darolutamide approved for CRPC and castration-sensitive disease. AR-V7 predicts ARSI resistance. RB1/TP53 co-loss drives AR-independent neuroendocrine transformation.

text_summary: The estrogen receptor (ER) signaling pathway is the primary oncogenic driver in approximately 70-80% of breast cancers. Estradiol (E2) binds ER-alpha (ESR1) in the cytoplasm, inducing receptor dimerization, nuclear translocation, and binding to estrogen response elements (EREs) to activate transcription of proliferative genes including CCND1 (cyclin D1), MYC, PGR, TFF1, and GREB1. ER signaling also activates non-genomic pathways through membrane-associated ER interacting with PI3K, MAPK, and Src kinase cascades. Endocrine therapies target multiple pathway nodes: selective estrogen receptor modulators (SERMs: tamoxifen), aromatase inhibitors (AIs: letrozole, anastrozole, exemestane, blocking estrogen synthesis), selective estrogen receptor degraders (SERDs: fulvestrant, elacestrant), and CDK4/6 inhibitors (blocking ER-driven cell cycle entry). ESR1 mutations (most commonly Y537S, D538G) emerge in approximately 30-40% of HR-positive metastatic breast cancers after AI exposure, conferring ligand-independent ER activity and AI resistance. Elacestrant, an oral SERD, is FDA-approved for ESR1-mutant, HR-positive/HER2-negative metastatic breast cancer after prior endocrine therapy. Cross-resistance between endocrine agents and pathway cross-talk with PI3K and MAPK drive combination strategies.
key_genes: ESR1, ESR2, CCND1, MYC, PGR, TFF1, GREB1, FOXA1, SRC, CYP19A1, NCOA3, SP1
therapeutic_targets: ER (tamoxifen, fulvestrant, elacestrant), aromatase (letrozole, anastrozole, exemestane), CDK4/6 (palbociclib, ribociclib, abemaciclib), PI3K (alpelisib), AKT (capivasertib)
cross_talk: PI3K/AKT/mTOR, MAPK/ERK, cell cycle (CDK4/6-RB), HER2/ErbB signaling, FGFR signaling
clinical_relevance: ER signaling drives ~75% of breast cancers. Endocrine therapy is foundation of HR+ breast cancer treatment. ESR1 mutations cause AI resistance (30-40% of metastatic HR+ BC). Elacestrant approved for ESR1-mutant disease. CDK4/6i + endocrine is standard 1L metastatic.

text_summary: The FGF/FGFR (Fibroblast Growth Factor/Fibroblast Growth Factor Receptor) signaling pathway comprises 22 FGF ligands and 4 FGFR tyrosine kinase receptors that regulate embryonic development, tissue repair, angiogenesis, and metabolism. FGF binding to FGFR (with heparan sulfate proteoglycan co-receptors) induces receptor dimerization and autophosphorylation, activating downstream MAPK/ERK, PI3K/AKT, PLCgamma, and STAT cascades. FGFR alterations in cancer include activating point mutations (FGFR2 in endometrial, FGFR3 in bladder), gene fusions (FGFR2 fusions in cholangiocarcinoma, FGFR3-TACC3 in glioblastoma and bladder), and amplifications (FGFR1 in squamous NSCLC and breast, FGFR2 in gastric). FDA-approved FGFR inhibitors include erdafitinib (pan-FGFR, approved for FGFR2/3-altered urothelial carcinoma), pemigatinib (FGFR1-3, approved for FGFR2-rearranged cholangiocarcinoma), futibatinib (irreversible pan-FGFR, approved for FGFR2-rearranged cholangiocarcinoma), and infigratinib (FGFR1-3, approved for FGFR2-rearranged cholangiocarcinoma). Resistance mechanisms include FGFR gatekeeper mutations (V555M), FGFR kinase domain mutations, and bypass pathway activation through MET or EGFR. FGF pathway dysregulation also contributes to resistance to other targeted therapies and immunotherapy.
key_genes: FGFR1, FGFR2, FGFR3, FGFR4, FGF3, FGF4, FGF10, FGF19, FRS2, PLCG1, GRB2, GAB1
therapeutic_targets: Pan-FGFR (erdafitinib, futibatinib), FGFR1-3 (pemigatinib, infigratinib), FGFR4-selective (fisogatinib, investigational)
cross_talk: MAPK/ERK, PI3K/AKT/mTOR, EGFR/ErbB signaling, MET signaling, angiogenesis/VEGF
clinical_relevance: FGFR2 fusions in 10-16% cholangiocarcinoma (3 approved drugs). FGFR2/3 alterations in ~20% urothelial (erdafitinib approved). FGFR1 amplification in squamous NSCLC. FGFR2 amplification in gastric (bemarituzumab). FGFR3 mutations in bladder cancer.

text_summary: The RAS/RAF/MEK resistance cascade describes the complex adaptive signaling mechanisms that emerge during treatment with MAPK pathway inhibitors, representing a major clinical challenge in precision oncology. Primary resistance mechanisms include concurrent genetic alterations (co-mutations in PIK3CA, PTEN loss, NF1 loss) that bypass MAPK dependence. Acquired resistance to BRAF inhibitors in melanoma involves BRAF amplification, BRAF splice variants (p61BRAF-V600E), MEK1/2 mutations (MEK1-C121S, MEK1-P124L), NRAS mutations, and RAF dimerization through CRAF overexpression. In BRAF-mutant colorectal cancer, intrinsic resistance to BRAF inhibitors is driven by EGFR-mediated feedback reactivation, necessitating the triple-combination of encorafenib plus cetuximab (plus or minus binimetinib). KRAS G12C inhibitor resistance involves acquisition of secondary KRAS mutations (Y96D, R68S), KRAS amplification, MET amplification, or histologic transformation. SHP2 inhibitors and SOS1 inhibitors are being developed to overcome RAS pathway resistance by blocking upstream adaptor signaling. ERK inhibitors (ulixertinib) directly target the terminal kinase, potentially overcoming MEK-level bypass. The adaptive resistance landscape underscores the need for combination strategies and serial molecular profiling during treatment.
key_genes: KRAS, NRAS, BRAF, MAP2K1, MAP2K2, MAPK1, NF1, PTPN11, SOS1, CRAF, ARAF, DUSP4, DUSP6, SPRY2
therapeutic_targets: SHP2 (SHP099, TNO155, investigational), SOS1 (BI-3406, investigational), ERK (ulixertinib, investigational), KRAS G12D (MRTX1133, investigational)
cross_talk: PI3K/AKT/mTOR (bypass), EGFR (feedback reactivation), MET (bypass amplification), cell cycle (CDK4/6)
clinical_relevance: BRAF inhibitor resistance in melanoma requires BRAF+MEK combination. CRC BRAF resistance requires BRAF+EGFR blockade. KRAS G12C inhibitor resistance emerging. Serial molecular profiling at progression recommended. Combination strategies targeting vertical and lateral pathways under investigation.

text_summary: The MYC/MAX transcriptional regulation pathway is one of the most commonly activated oncogenic programs in cancer, with MYC (c-MYC) amplification, overexpression, or translocation found in an estimated 70% of all cancers. MYC heterodimerizes with MAX through their basic helix-loop-helix leucine zipper (bHLHLZ) domains to bind E-box DNA elements (CACGTG) and activate transcription of thousands of genes controlling cell proliferation (CDK4, CCND2), ribosome biogenesis, metabolism (LDHA, GLS), and stem cell self-renewal. MYC family members (MYC, MYCN, MYCL) have distinct tissue-specific oncogenic roles: MYC in Burkitt lymphoma (t(8;14) translocation), DLBCL, breast, and NSCLC; MYCN in neuroblastoma (amplification defines high-risk disease) and SCLC; MYCL in SCLC. MYC has historically been considered undruggable due to its intrinsically disordered protein structure and essential role in normal cells. However, emerging therapeutic strategies include MYC/MAX dimerization inhibitors (Omomyc-derived peptidomimetics), BET bromodomain inhibitors (JQ1, birabresib) that suppress MYC transcription, CDK7/9 inhibitors that block MYC-dependent transcription elongation, and degradation approaches (PROTACs). MYC cooperates with BCL-2 to create double-hit lymphomas with particularly aggressive biology.
key_genes: MYC, MYCN, MYCL, MAX, MXD1, MNT, MAD, CDK9, BRD4, CCND2, CDK4, LDHA, GLS, TERT
therapeutic_targets: BET bromodomain (JQ1, birabresib, investigational), CDK7 (samuraciclib, investigational), CDK9 (investigational), MYC/MAX disruptors (Omomyc, investigational)
cross_talk: Cell cycle (CDK4/6-RB), PI3K/AKT/mTOR, WNT/beta-catenin, MAPK/ERK, apoptosis (BCL-2), metabolic reprogramming
clinical_relevance: MYC amplified/overexpressed in ~70% of cancers. MYC translocation in Burkitt lymphoma. Double-hit lymphoma (MYC+BCL2) poor prognosis. MYCN amplification defines high-risk neuroblastoma. MYC considered historically undruggable; emerging indirect targeting strategies.

text_summary: Epigenetic regulation through DNA methylation, governed by DNMT3A, TET2, and IDH1/2 enzymes, is a fundamental mechanism of gene expression control frequently disrupted in cancer, particularly in hematologic malignancies. DNA methyltransferases (DNMT1 for maintenance methylation, DNMT3A/3B for de novo methylation) add methyl groups to cytosine at CpG dinucleotides, generally silencing gene expression. TET family enzymes (TET1, TET2, TET3) catalyze the oxidation of 5-methylcytosine to 5-hydroxymethylcytosine, initiating active DNA demethylation. DNMT3A mutations occur in approximately 20-25% of AML and are frequently early clonal events associated with clonal hematopoiesis of indeterminate potential (CHIP). TET2 mutations (10-15% of AML, 10-20% of MDS) impair DNA demethylation, leading to hypermethylation of tumor suppressor gene promoters. IDH1/2 mutations produce the oncometabolite D-2-hydroxyglutarate, which competitively inhibits TET2 and other alpha-ketoglutarate-dependent dioxygenases, creating a hypermethylation phenotype. Hypomethylating agents (azacitidine, decitabine) incorporate into DNA and trap DNMTs, leading to passive demethylation and re-expression of silenced genes. These agents are the backbone of therapy for MDS and unfit AML.
key_genes: DNMT1, DNMT3A, DNMT3B, TET1, TET2, TET3, IDH1, IDH2, ASXL1, WT1, CEBPA, MECOM
therapeutic_targets: DNMT (azacitidine, decitabine, oral decitabine/cedazuridine), IDH1 (ivosidenib), IDH2 (enasidenib), menin (revumenib, investigational)
cross_talk: Histone modification (EZH2/PRC2), chromatin remodeling (SWI/SNF), cell cycle (CDK4/6), apoptosis (BCL-2), IDH metabolic pathway
clinical_relevance: DNMT3A/TET2 mutations are early CHIP events and the most common mutations in AML/MDS. Hypomethylating agents are the standard backbone for unfit AML and MDS. IDH1/2 inhibitors approved for AML. DNMT3A R882H is the most common mutation. TET2 mutations impair anti-tumor immunity.

text_summary: RNA splicing regulation is a critical post-transcriptional process frequently disrupted in myeloid malignancies through recurrent mutations in spliceosome components. SF3B1, SRSF2, U2AF1, and ZRSR2 are the four most commonly mutated splicing factor genes, collectively mutated in approximately 50-60% of myelodysplastic syndromes (MDS) and 10-15% of AML. SF3B1 mutations (K700E being most common) are strongly associated with MDS with ring sideroblasts (MDS-RS) and are defined as a favorable-risk entity in the WHO classification. SRSF2 mutations (P95H/L/R) alter splice site recognition specificity and co-occur with IDH2 mutations and TET2 mutations, defining a specific AML/MDS subgroup. U2AF1 mutations (S34F, Q157P/R) affect the recognition of 3-prime splice sites and are associated with adverse prognosis. These mutations cause widespread mis-splicing, generating aberrant transcript isoforms that drive disease pathogenesis through disrupted hematopoietic differentiation. Therapeutic targeting of the spliceosome is under active investigation, with SF3B1 inhibitors (H3B-8800) and modulation of specific mis-spliced targets being explored. The 2022 ELN classification incorporates SRSF2, SF3B1, U2AF1, and ZRSR2 mutation status into AML risk stratification.
key_genes: SF3B1, SRSF2, U2AF1, ZRSR2, SF3A1, PRPF8, DDX41, LUC7L2, PRPF40B, RBM10
therapeutic_targets: SF3B1 (H3B-8800, investigational), spliceosome modulators (E7107, pladienolide B, investigational), downstream mis-spliced targets
cross_talk: DNA methylation (TET2, IDH2 co-mutations with SRSF2), DNA damage response (DDX41), chromatin remodeling (ASXL1), apoptosis (BCL-2)
clinical_relevance: Splicing factor mutations in ~50-60% MDS and ~15% AML. SF3B1 defines MDS-RS (favorable). SRSF2/U2AF1 associated with adverse prognosis in AML. Spliceosome mutations are mutually exclusive. H3B-8800 spliceosome inhibitor in clinical trials.

text_summary: The telomere maintenance pathway centered on TERT (telomerase reverse transcriptase) is a fundamental mechanism by which cancer cells achieve replicative immortality, one of the hallmarks of cancer. Telomeres, the repetitive TTAGGG sequences capping chromosome ends, shorten with each cell division in normal somatic cells, eventually triggering senescence or apoptosis. Cancer cells bypass this mortality checkpoint primarily through TERT reactivation (85-90% of cancers) or the ALT (Alternative Lengthening of Telomeres) recombination pathway (10-15%, common in sarcomas, gliomas, and neuroendocrine tumors). TERT promoter mutations (C228T and C250T, creating de novo ETS transcription factor binding sites) are highly prevalent in melanoma (70-80%), glioblastoma (80%), hepatocellular carcinoma (60%), urothelial carcinoma (60-80%), and thyroid cancer (40-50%). TERT promoter mutations are mutually exclusive with ALT and correlate with adverse prognosis in several cancer types. TERT promoter mutation status is being incorporated into diagnostic and prognostic algorithms for thyroid cancer, glioma, and melanoma. Therapeutic targeting of telomerase includes imetelstat (telomerase inhibitor approved for lower-risk MDS), TERT peptide vaccines, and G-quadruplex stabilizers that block telomeric DNA elongation.
key_genes: TERT, TERC, DKC1, POT1, TRF1, TRF2, RAP1, ATRX, DAXX, TPP1, TIN2
therapeutic_targets: Telomerase (imetelstat), TERT vaccines (investigational), G-quadruplex stabilizers (investigational), ATRX-targeting for ALT tumors (investigational)
cross_talk: DNA damage response (ATM/ATR at uncapped telomeres), cell cycle (p53/RB senescence), chromatin remodeling (ATRX/DAXX for ALT), MYC (TERT transcription)
clinical_relevance: TERT promoter mutations in melanoma, glioblastoma, HCC, urothelial, thyroid cancers. Imetelstat approved for lower-risk MDS. ALT pathway in sarcomas and gliomas (ATRX loss). TERT promoter mutation prognostic in thyroid cancer and glioma. Replicative immortality is a universal cancer hallmark.

text_summary: The autophagy pathway is a highly conserved cellular recycling mechanism that degrades damaged organelles and protein aggregates through lysosomal degradation, playing a complex dual role in cancer as both a tumor suppressor (early stages) and a tumor promoter (advanced disease). The core autophagy machinery involves the ULK1/ATG13/FIP200 initiation complex, the Beclin-1/VPS34 PI3K-III nucleation complex, and the ATG5-ATG12-ATG16L1 and LC3 conjugation systems that form the autophagosome membrane. In established tumors, autophagy sustains cancer cell survival under metabolic stress (nutrient deprivation, hypoxia), supports drug resistance by degrading damaged cellular components, and maintains cancer stem cell populations. KRAS-mutant cancers (pancreatic, NSCLC, colorectal) are particularly dependent on autophagy for metabolic homeostasis. Hydroxychloroquine (HCQ), a lysosomotropic agent that blocks autophagosome-lysosome fusion, is being studied in combination with MEK inhibitors, BRAF inhibitors, and chemotherapy in KRAS-mutant pancreatic cancer. The PAX-002 trial demonstrated that HCQ combined with trametinib has activity in KRAS-mutant pancreatic cancer. More potent and specific autophagy inhibitors targeting ULK1 (DCC-3116) and VPS34 are in development. Paradoxically, autophagy also mediates immunogenic cell death and antigen presentation, making its therapeutic modulation context-dependent.
key_genes: ULK1, ATG13, FIP200, BECN1, VPS34, ATG5, ATG7, ATG12, ATG16L1, MAP1LC3B, SQSTM1, LAMP2, STK11, AMPK
therapeutic_targets: Lysosome (hydroxychloroquine), ULK1 (DCC-3116, investigational), VPS34 (investigational), ATG4B (investigational)
cross_talk: PI3K/AKT/mTOR (mTORC1 inhibits autophagy), AMPK/STK11 (activates autophagy), MAPK/ERK (KRAS-driven autophagy), p53 (transcriptional activation), apoptosis (BCL-2/Beclin-1 interaction)
clinical_relevance: KRAS-mutant cancers are autophagy-dependent. HCQ + trametinib in clinical trials for pancreatic cancer. Autophagy supports drug resistance and cancer stem cells. ULK1 inhibitors in early development. Context-dependent role complicates therapeutic targeting.

text_summary: The unfolded protein response (UPR) is an endoplasmic reticulum (ER) stress signaling network that cancer cells exploit to survive under conditions of proteotoxic stress, hypoxia, nutrient deprivation, and high biosynthetic demand. Three ER-resident sensors initiate the UPR: IRE1alpha (ERN1), PERK (EIF2AK3), and ATF6. IRE1alpha activates the XBP1 transcription factor through unconventional mRNA splicing and has kinase activity that can activate JNK/MAPK signaling. PERK phosphorylates eIF2alpha to globally suppress translation while selectively upregulating ATF4 and CHOP (DDIT3). ATF6 is cleaved in the Golgi to release an active transcription factor. The UPR is constitutively activated in multiple myeloma (high immunoglobulin secretory burden), chronic lymphocytic leukemia, and many solid tumors with high proliferative and secretory demands. Proteasome inhibitors (bortezomib, carfilzomib, ixazomib) exploit the UPR dependence of myeloma by blocking proteasomal degradation of misfolded proteins, overwhelming the ER protein folding capacity and triggering terminal UPR-mediated apoptosis. IRE1alpha inhibitors and PERK modulators are under investigation as cancer therapies. The UPR also modulates tumor immunogenicity through calreticulin exposure during immunogenic cell death.
key_genes: ERN1, EIF2AK3, ATF6, XBP1, ATF4, DDIT3, HSPA5, HSP90B1, CALR, CANX, EDEM1, PDIA3
therapeutic_targets: Proteasome (bortezomib, carfilzomib, ixazomib), IRE1alpha (MKC-8866, investigational), PERK (GSK2606414, investigational), HSP90 (investigational)
cross_talk: Apoptosis (CHOP/DDIT3-mediated), autophagy (UPR activates autophagy), NF-kB (IRE1alpha-TRAF2), PI3K/AKT (survival signaling), tumor immune microenvironment (calreticulin exposure)
clinical_relevance: UPR exploitation by proteasome inhibitors is the foundation of myeloma therapy. Bortezomib/carfilzomib/ixazomib induce terminal UPR. IRE1alpha/XBP1 pathway essential in myeloma and CLL. Calreticulin exposure mediates immunogenic cell death. PERK inhibitors in preclinical development.

text_summary: The SWI/SNF (BAF/PBAF) chromatin remodeling complex is a multi-subunit ATP-dependent nucleosome remodeling machine that uses the energy of ATP hydrolysis to slide, eject, or restructure nucleosomes, thereby regulating chromatin accessibility and gene expression. SWI/SNF complex subunits are collectively mutated in approximately 20% of all human cancers, making them among the most frequently altered gene families in oncology. Key subunits and their cancer associations include: SMARCB1/INI1 (malignant rhabdoid tumors, epithelioid sarcoma), SMARCA4/BRG1 (NSCLC, ovarian SCCOHT, thoracic sarcomas), ARID1A (ovarian clear cell 46-57%, endometrial 30-40%, gastric 8-27%), PBRM1 (clear cell renal cell carcinoma 40%), ARID2 (hepatocellular carcinoma, melanoma), and BAP1 (mesothelioma, uveal melanoma, cholangiocarcinoma). Loss of SWI/SNF function creates therapeutic vulnerabilities through synthetic lethality with the PRC2 complex (EZH2 dependency), residual SWI/SNF paralog dependency (SMARCA2/BRM dependence in SMARCA4-deficient cancers), and altered DNA damage repair. Tazemetostat (EZH2 inhibitor) is approved for SMARCB1-negative epithelioid sarcoma. SMARCA2/BRM degraders are in clinical development for SMARCA4-mutant cancers. PBRM1 loss in RCC may predict benefit from immune checkpoint inhibitors.
key_genes: SMARCB1, SMARCA4, SMARCA2, ARID1A, ARID1B, PBRM1, ARID2, BAP1, BRD7, DPF2, SMARCD1, SMARCE1, SMARCC1, SMARCC2
therapeutic_targets: EZH2 (tazemetostat, for SWI/SNF-deficient tumors), SMARCA2/BRM degraders (FHD-286, PRT3789, investigational), ATR (investigational in ARID1A-deficient), PARP (investigational in ARID1A-deficient)
cross_talk: EZH2/PRC2 (antagonistic balance), DNA damage response, PI3K/AKT (ARID1A regulates PI3K), ER signaling (ARID1A modulates ER transcription), p53 pathway
clinical_relevance: SWI/SNF mutations in ~20% of all cancers. Tazemetostat for SMARCB1-negative epithelioid sarcoma. ARID1A loss in ovarian clear cell/endometrial. PBRM1 loss in RCC (potential IO biomarker). SMARCA4 loss defines thoracic sarcoma entity. Synthetic lethality strategies under development.

text_summary: The complement and innate immunity pathway encompasses a cascade of serum proteins and cellular effectors that serve as the first line of host defense and have been increasingly recognized as modulators of cancer progression and anti-tumor immunity. The complement system activates through three pathways (classical, lectin, and alternative), converging on C3 convertase to generate C3a and C5a anaphylatoxins and the C5b-9 membrane attack complex. In the tumor microenvironment, complement activation has paradoxical effects: complement can mediate anti-tumor cytotoxicity (CDC by anti-CD20 antibodies like rituximab), but tumor-generated C3a and C5a also promote immunosuppression by recruiting myeloid-derived suppressor cells (MDSCs), polarizing macrophages to the M2 phenotype, and suppressing T-cell function. Tumors express complement regulatory proteins (CD46, CD55, CD59) to resist complement-mediated killing. C5a receptor antagonists are under investigation as immunomodulators in combination with checkpoint inhibitors. The innate immune system additionally contributes through natural killer (NK) cell surveillance (mediated by NKG2D, KIR, and CD16 for ADCC), dendritic cell antigen presentation, and macrophage phagocytosis. Anti-CD47/SIRPalpha checkpoint (macrophage phagocytosis checkpoint) inhibitors are in clinical development across hematologic and solid malignancies.
key_genes: C3, C5, C5AR1, C5AR2, CD46, CD55, CD59, CFH, CFB, CFD, CD47, SIRPA, FCGR3A, KLRK1, NCR1
therapeutic_targets: CD47 (magrolimab, investigational), SIRPalpha (evorpacept, investigational), C5aR (vilobelimab, investigational), complement (eculizumab for paroxysmal nocturnal hemoglobinuria)
cross_talk: Adaptive immunity (complement bridges innate and adaptive), NF-kB, cGAS-STING, tumor immune microenvironment, coagulation cascade
clinical_relevance: CDC mechanism for rituximab. CD47/SIRPalpha checkpoint in clinical trials (magrolimab in MDS/AML). Complement regulatory proteins protect tumors. NK cell therapies emerging. ADCC mechanism for trastuzumab and cetuximab. C5a promotes immunosuppressive TME.

text_summary: The adenosine/purinergic signaling pathway is an immunosuppressive mechanism in the tumor microenvironment whereby extracellular ATP released from dying cells is sequentially dephosphorylated by the ectonucleotidases CD73 (NT5E, converting AMP to adenosine) and CD39 (ENTPD1, converting ATP to ADP/AMP), generating high concentrations of extracellular adenosine that signals through A2A (ADORA2A) and A2B (ADORA2B) adenosine receptors on immune cells. Adenosine signaling through A2AR on T cells suppresses their proliferation, cytokine production, and cytotoxic function, while promoting regulatory T-cell differentiation and myeloid-derived suppressor cell accumulation. Hypoxia-inducible factor (HIF-1alpha) transcriptionally upregulates both CD73 and A2BR in the hypoxic tumor microenvironment, amplifying immunosuppression. The CD73-adenosine-A2AR axis represents a major immune checkpoint being targeted therapeutically: anti-CD73 antibodies (oleclumab), A2AR antagonists (ciforadenant, taminadenant), dual CD73/A2AR inhibitors, and anti-CD39 antibodies are in clinical trials, frequently in combination with anti-PD-1/PD-L1 therapy. High CD73 expression on tumor cells is associated with worse prognosis and immunotherapy resistance in NSCLC, TNBC, ovarian, and colorectal cancers. The pathway also promotes angiogenesis and tumor cell migration through A2BR signaling.
key_genes: NT5E, ENTPD1, ADORA2A, ADORA2B, ADORA1, HIF1A, PANX1, P2RX7, ADA, ENPP1
therapeutic_targets: CD73 (oleclumab, investigational), A2AR (ciforadenant, taminadenant, investigational), CD39 (TTX-030, investigational), dual CD73/A2AR (investigational)
cross_talk: Tumor immune microenvironment, HIF/hypoxia signaling, cGAS-STING (ENPP1 degrades cGAMP), angiogenesis/VEGF, PD-1/PD-L1 checkpoint
clinical_relevance: CD73-adenosine-A2AR axis is a major immunosuppressive mechanism. High CD73 predicts poor prognosis and IO resistance. Anti-CD73 + anti-PD-L1 combinations in clinical trials (COAST, PACIFIC-9). ENPP1 also degrades cGAMP (linking to STING suppression). Emerging therapeutic target in immuno-oncology.

text_summary: FGFR4-mediated hepatocyte signaling represents a tissue-specific oncogenic pathway driven by the FGF19-FGFR4-KLB axis that is uniquely relevant to hepatocellular carcinoma (HCC). FGF19 (the human ortholog of murine FGF15) is an endocrine FGF produced by ileal enterocytes in response to bile acid absorption, signaling through FGFR4 with the obligate co-receptor beta-Klotho (KLB) on hepatocytes to regulate bile acid synthesis, glucose metabolism, and hepatocyte proliferation. In HCC, FGF19 amplification or overexpression (present in approximately 15-20% of HCC) constitutively activates the FGFR4-KLB axis, driving hepatocyte proliferation through MAPK/ERK and PI3K/AKT signaling and suppressing bile acid synthesis through CYP7A1 repression. FGFR4-selective inhibitors (fisogatinib/BLU-554, roblitinib/FGF401) have been developed to spare FGFR1-3, avoiding the significant off-target toxicities (hyperphosphatemia, tissue calcification) seen with pan-FGFR inhibitors. Clinical trials of FGFR4-selective inhibitors in FGF19-positive HCC have shown modest single-agent activity (ORR 16-17%), and combination strategies with checkpoint inhibitors are being explored. The pathway also has relevance to cholestasis, gallbladder cancer, and metabolic syndrome.
key_genes: FGFR4, FGF19, KLB, FRS2, CYP7A1, SHP, GRB2, MAPK1, AKT1, STAT3
therapeutic_targets: FGFR4-selective (fisogatinib/BLU-554, roblitinib/FGF401, investigational), pan-FGFR (futibatinib), FGF19 antibody (investigational)
cross_talk: MAPK/ERK, PI3K/AKT, bile acid metabolism, WNT/beta-catenin, HIF/angiogenesis in HCC
clinical_relevance: FGF19 amplification/overexpression in 15-20% HCC. FGFR4-selective inhibitors in clinical trials. Unique hepatocyte-specific pathway. FGF19 is both metabolic regulator and oncogenic driver. Combination with IO under investigation. Avoids hyperphosphatemia of pan-FGFR inhibition.

text_summary: B-cell receptor (BCR) signaling is the central survival and proliferative pathway in mature B-cell lymphomas, including chronic lymphocytic leukemia (CLL), mantle cell lymphoma (MCL), marginal zone lymphoma (MZL), and diffuse large B-cell lymphoma (DLBCL, particularly the ABC subtype). Antigen-dependent or tonic (antigen-independent) BCR stimulation activates a signaling cascade through the immunoreceptor tyrosine-based activation motifs (ITAMs) of CD79A/B, recruiting SYK kinase, which activates BTK (Bruton tyrosine kinase) and PI3K-delta. BTK phosphorylates PLCgamma2, generating IP3 and DAG to activate calcium signaling and PKC, respectively, leading to NF-kB, NFAT, and MAPK transcription factor activation. Constitutive BCR signaling in lymphomas results from chronic antigen stimulation (CLL), activating mutations in CD79A/B (ABC-DLBCL), or downstream mutations (CARD11, MYD88 L265P in Waldenstrom macroglobulinemia). BTK inhibitors (ibrutinib, acalabrutinib, zanubrutinib, pirtobrutinib) have revolutionized B-cell lymphoma treatment by disrupting this central survival signal. PI3K-delta inhibitors (idelalisib, duvelisib, copanlisib) target the PI3K arm of BCR signaling. Resistance to covalent BTK inhibitors occurs through BTK C481S mutations, addressed by non-covalent BTK inhibitors (pirtobrutinib) and BTK degraders (NX-2127).
key_genes: CD79A, CD79B, SYK, BTK, PLCG2, PRKCB, CARD11, BCL10, MALT1, BLNK, LYN, PIK3CD, MYD88, IKBKB
therapeutic_targets: BTK (ibrutinib, acalabrutinib, zanubrutinib, pirtobrutinib), PI3K-delta (idelalisib, copanlisib), SYK (fostamatinib), BTK degraders (NX-2127, investigational)
cross_talk: NF-kB (CARD11/BCL10/MALT1), PI3K/AKT/mTOR, MAPK/ERK, calcium/NFAT, apoptosis (BCL-2), JAK/STAT
clinical_relevance: BTK inhibitors transformed CLL, MCL, WM treatment. BCR signaling is the central survival pathway in B-cell lymphomas. CD79B mutations in ABC-DLBCL. MYD88 L265P in WM (ibrutinib approved). BTK C481S resistance mechanism. Pirtobrutinib for covalent BTKi-resistant CLL. PI3K-delta inhibitors in indolent lymphomas.
